questions.questionText: Coagulative necrosis is most commonly seen in:
questions.optionB: All organs EXCEPT the brain (most commonly in ischemic injury to solid organs)
questions.explanation: Coagulative necrosis: most common type, occurs in ischemic injury to solid organs (heart, kidney, liver, spleen). Tissue architecture is preserved ('ghost outlines') due to protein denaturation. EXCEPTION: brain undergoes liquefactive necrosis (even with ischemia — due to high lipid content and hydrolytic enzymes). Caseous necrosis: TB (granulomas). Fat necrosis: pancreas (enzymatic), breast (traumatic). Fibrinoid necrosis: blood vessel walls in vasculitis, malignant HTN. Reference: Robbins — Cell Injury.

questions.questionText: Apoptosis differs from necrosis in that apoptosis:
questions.optionB: Is programmed cell death with cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, apoptotic bodies, and NO inflammation
questions.explanation: Apoptosis: programmed cell death. Features: cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation/margination, cytoplasmic blebs, apoptotic bodies (phagocytosed), intact cell membrane → NO inflammation. Pathways: intrinsic (mitochondrial — BCL-2 family, cytochrome c release → caspase-9) and extrinsic (death receptor — Fas/FasL, TNF → caspase-8). Both activate caspase-3 (executioner). Necrosis: cell swelling, membrane rupture, inflammation. Reference: Robbins — Apoptosis.

questions.optionB: Reversible change in which one differentiated cell type is replaced by another differentiated cell type
questions.explanation: Metaplasia: reversible replacement of one mature cell type by another mature cell type (reprogramming of stem cells). Examples: squamous metaplasia of respiratory epithelium (smoking), Barrett's esophagus (squamous → intestinal columnar — due to GERD), apocrine metaplasia of breast, osseous metaplasia. Significance: adaptive but may predispose to dysplasia/neoplasia (Barrett's → adenocarcinoma). Metaplasia is NOT direct transformation of existing cells. Reference: Robbins — Cellular Adaptations.

questions.questionText: The 'point of no return' in irreversible cell injury is marked by:
questions.optionB: Mitochondrial membrane damage and massive calcium influx
questions.explanation: Irreversible cell injury markers: (1) Severe mitochondrial damage (loss of membrane potential, mitochondrial permeability transition, inability to generate ATP), (2) Massive calcium influx (activates phospholipases, proteases, endonucleases), (3) Plasma membrane damage (leakage of intracellular contents). Reversible injury features: cell swelling (hydropic change), fatty change, ER dilation, ribosomal detachment, myelin figures. Nuclear changes in necrosis: pyknosis → karyorrhexis → karyolysis. Reference: Robbins — Irreversible Injury.

questions.optionB: Dead or dying tissues with NORMAL calcium levels
questions.explanation: Dystrophic calcification: calcium deposition in ABNORMAL (necrotic/damaged) tissues with NORMAL serum calcium. Examples: atherosclerotic plaques, damaged heart valves, TB granulomas (caseous necrosis), fat necrosis, psammoma bodies (papillary thyroid/serous ovarian/meningioma). Metastatic calcification: calcium deposition in NORMAL tissues due to HYPERCALCEMIA (hyperparathyroidism, renal failure, vitamin D excess, bone destruction). Reference: Robbins — Pathologic Calcification.

questions.questionText: Free radical injury to cells occurs through:
questions.optionB: Lipid peroxidation of membranes, protein/DNA damage, and oxidative stress
questions.explanation: Free radicals (ROS — superoxide O2•−, hydroxyl OH•, H2O2): highly reactive, unpaired electron. Mechanisms of injury: (1) Lipid peroxidation of cell membranes (chain reaction → membrane damage), (2) Protein oxidation/cross-linking, (3) DNA strand breaks. Antioxidant defenses: SOD (O2•− → H2O2), catalase/glutathione peroxidase (H2O2 → H2O), vitamins E, C, A. Generated in: reperfusion injury, radiation, chemical toxins, inflammation (neutrophil respiratory burst). Reference: Robbins — Free Radical Injury.

questions.optionB: Increase in cell SIZE (not number), leading to organ enlargement
questions.explanation: Hypertrophy: increase in CELL SIZE → organ enlargement. No new cells. Occurs in cells that cannot divide (cardiac myocytes, skeletal muscle). Physiological: uterus in pregnancy (hormonal), bodybuilder muscles (increased workload). Pathological: left ventricular hypertrophy (hypertension — pressure overload), right ventricular hypertrophy (pulmonary HTN). Hyperplasia: increase in cell NUMBER. Both can coexist (uterus in pregnancy: both hypertrophy and hyperplasia). Reference: Robbins — Cellular Adaptations.

questions.optionB: 'Wear and tear' endogenous pigment (lipid peroxidation product in aging cells)
questions.explanation: Lipofuscin: golden-brown intracellular granular pigment (lipid peroxidation residual bodies from autophagy) — 'wear and tear pigment.' Accumulates in aging cells — heart, liver, brain. Does NOT impair cell function. Sign of free radical injury over time. Other endogenous pigments: melanin (brown-black, melanocytes), hemosiderin (golden-brown, iron from Hb breakdown — Prussian blue positive), bilirubin (yellow, Hb degradation). Exogenous pigments: carbon (anthracosis), tattoo ink. Reference: Robbins — Intracellular Accumulations.

questions.questionText: Caseous necrosis is most characteristically seen in:
questions.explanation: Caseous necrosis: 'cheese-like' friable yellowish-white material. Characteristic of TB (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) — seen in the center of granulomas. Microscopically: amorphous, acellular eosinophilic debris with complete loss of tissue architecture (unlike coagulative necrosis). Also seen in: fungal infections (histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis). Langhans giant cells and epithelioid cells surround the caseous center. Reference: Robbins — Caseous Necrosis.

questions.optionB: An anti-apoptotic protein (prevents cytochrome c release from mitochondria)
questions.explanation: BCL-2: anti-apoptotic protein located on outer mitochondrial membrane → prevents cytochrome c release → blocks apoptosis. Overexpressed in: follicular lymphoma (t(14;18) translocation — BCL2 gene next to IgH promoter). Pro-apoptotic BCL-2 family members: BAX, BAK (form pores → cytochrome c release → caspase activation). Intrinsic pathway: cellular stress → increased BAX/BAK, decreased BCL-2 → cytochrome c → apoptosome → caspase-9 → caspase-3. Reference: Robbins — BCL-2 Family.

questions.questionText: Fatty change (steatosis) is most commonly seen in the:
questions.explanation: Fatty change: reversible accumulation of triglycerides in parenchymal cells. Most common in liver (central role in lipid metabolism). Causes: alcohol (most common cause), diabetes, obesity, malnutrition/starvation, hypoxia, hepatotoxins (CCl4), pregnancy. Macrovesicular (large droplet) or microvesicular (small droplets — acute fatty liver of pregnancy, Reye syndrome). Grossly: enlarged, yellow, greasy liver. Mild: reversible with removal of cause. Reference: Robbins — Steatosis.

questions.optionB: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), complement activation, and neutrophil influx upon restoration of blood flow
questions.explanation: Reperfusion injury: paradoxical worsening of cell damage when blood flow is restored to ischemic tissue. Mechanisms: (1) Burst of ROS generation (xanthine oxidase, mitochondria, neutrophils), (2) Complement activation, (3) Neutrophil infiltration (release proteases, elastase, ROS), (4) Mitochondrial permeability transition (mPTP opening). Clinically important in: MI (post-thrombolysis/PCI), stroke, organ transplantation, cardiac surgery. Reference: Robbins — Reperfusion Injury.

questions.optionB: Abnormally folded fibrillar proteins (β-pleated sheet configuration, Congo red birefringence)
questions.explanation: Amyloidosis: extracellular deposition of misfolded proteins in β-pleated sheet configuration. Stain: Congo red → apple-green birefringence under polarized light. Types: AL (light chain — primary/myeloma-associated), AA (serum amyloid A — secondary/reactive in chronic inflammation), ATTR (transthyretin — senile cardiac, familial), Aβ2M (dialysis), Aβ (Alzheimer). Organ involvement: kidney (nephrotic syndrome), heart (restrictive CMP), liver, spleen, GI. Reference: Robbins — Amyloidosis.

questions.optionB: Disordered cell growth with loss of uniformity and architectural orientation (pre-malignant)
questions.explanation: Dysplasia: disordered cell growth with pleomorphism (variation in size/shape), hyperchromatic nuclei, increased mitotic figures (including atypical mitoses), loss of normal maturation and orientation. Pre-malignant but reversible if stimulus removed. Often seen in: cervical epithelium (CIN — cervical intraepithelial neoplasia), Barrett's esophagus, bronchial epithelium (smokers). Severe dysplasia = carcinoma in situ (full thickness involvement without invasion through basement membrane). Reference: Robbins — Dysplasia.

questions.questionText: Autophagy is a cellular process that:
questions.optionB: Degrades and recycles damaged organelles and misfolded proteins via lysosomal digestion
questions.explanation: Autophagy: cellular self-eating — cytoplasmic contents (damaged organelles, misfolded proteins) sequestered in autophagosomes → fuse with lysosomes (autolysosomes) → degradation → recycling of building blocks. Functions: nutrient recycling during starvation, quality control (removing damaged mitochondria — mitophagy), defense against pathogens. Regulated by mTOR (inhibits autophagy when nutrients are abundant). Defective autophagy: neurodegeneration, cancer, aging. Reference: Robbins — Autophagy.
